coredata.dc:title: Portable and writable photoluminescent chalk for on-site information protection on arbitrary substrates
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Fei-Fei
Zhu, Ying-Jie
Zhang, Yong-Gang
Yang, Ri-Long
Yu, Han-Ping
Qin, Dong-Dong
Xiong, Zhi-Chao
coredata.dc:description: Information protection is particularly important for secure communication between parties, avoiding the leakage of secret information to unknown parties. Among various strategies used for information protection in the previous reports in the literature, security inks made from photoluminescent materials are the most widely investigated. The security inks suffer from the drawbacks such as lack of universality on various substrates. In this work, we provide a promising new complement to the security ink-based protection techniques. We report a kind of portable photoluminescent chalk made from lanthanide-doped ultralong hydroxyapatite nanowires, which can directly write the covert information at anytime and anyplace on arbitrary surfaces and substrates including paper sheets, metals, fabrics, plastics, woods, walls, foams, leaves, and even human body. The as-written original information cannot be recognized under the ambient light, but it is readable upon exposure to the ultraviolet light. In this work, the dual and triple encryption strategies are also provided for the high-level information protection. It is expected that the versatile, convenient, and user-friendly strategy demonstrated herein is promising for the on-site secret information protection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hydroxyapatite
Nanowire
Luminescence
Chalk
Data encryption

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Novel Materials for Energy and Advanced Applications

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a–c) Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the as-prepared ultralong nanowires: (a) HAP, (b) HAP:Eu3+, and (c) HAP:Tb3+. (d–f) Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of the surface of the performed PL chalk after pressure molding: (d) HAP, (e) HAP:Eu3+, and (f) HAP:Tb3+. (g–i) SEM images of the surface of the PL chalk after sintering at 1000 °C: (g) HAP, (h) HAP:Eu3+, and (i) HAP:Tb3+.

(a) XRD patterns, (b) FTIR spectra, and (c–e) XPS spectra of the as-prepared ultralong nanowires of HAP, HAP:Eu3+, and HAP:Tb3+ without sintering. Note: different colored curves stand for different samples in (a-e), as indicated in the top right-hand corner diagram.

(a) XRD patterns, (b) FTIR spectra, and (c–e) EDS spectra of the as-prepared HAP, HAP:Eu3+, and HAP:Tb3+ chalks after sintering at 1000 °C. Note: different colored curves stand for different samples in (a-e), as indicated in the top right-hand corner diagram.

(a, b) Photoluminescence excitation spectra of the HAP:Eu3+ and HAP:Tb3+ PL chalks after sintering at 1000 °C under the emission wavelength of 616 and 545 nm, respectively. (c, d) Photoluminescence emission spectra of the HAP:Eu3+ and HAP:Tb3+ PL chalks after sintering at 1000 °C under the excitation wavelength of 393 and 377 nm, respectively.

The as-prepared HAP:Eu3+ and HAP:Tb3+ chalks after sintering under the ambient light (a) and 254 nm UV light (b). (c, d) The double PL chalk after sintering under the ambient light (c) and 254 nm UV light (d). (e) The PL chalk can be packed into the sleeve to carry conveniently. (f) The PL chalk is writable on arbitrary substrates and surfaces.

Various substrates with English letters written using the as-prepared PL chalk under the ambient light (upper panel) and 254 nm UV light (lower panel): (a) paper, (b) metal, (c) fabric, (d) plastic, (e) wood, (f) wall, (g) foam, (h) leaf, and (i) human hand.

(a-c) The encrypted hidden information written by using the as-prepared PL chalk under the ambient light (upper panel) and 254 nm UV light (lower panel): (a) flower, (b) map, and (c) ten numbers. (d) The words “HELLO THERE” are dual-encrypted by scrambled letters. (e) The information of “SICCAS” is triple-encrypted with the aid of ASCII binary codes.

Schematic illustration for portable and writable photoluminescent chalk for the on-site information protection on arbitrary substrates: (a) the as-prepared PL chalks made from HAP:Eu3+ and HAP:Tb3+ nanowires emit different fluorescence colors under the UV light; (b) the users can directly write the confidential information on arbitrary substrates using the PL chalk; (c) one of design strategies for storing the secret information using two kinds of PL chalks.

head.ce:title: Portable and writable photoluminescent chalk for on-site information protection on arbitrary substrates

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of High Performance Ceramics and Superfine Microstructure, Shanghai Institute of Ceramics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Shanghai 200050, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of High Performance Ceramics and Superfine Microstructure

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center of Materials Science and Optoelectronics Engineering, University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100049, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Center of Materials Science and Optoelectronics Engineering
University of Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: State Key Laboratory of High Performance Ceramics and Superfine Microstructure, Shanghai Institute of Ceramics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Shanghai 200050, PR China (Y.-J. Zhu).

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of High Performance Ceramics and Superfine Microstructure

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Information protection is particularly important for secure communication between parties, avoiding the leakage of secret information to unknown parties. Among various strategies used for information protection in the previous reports in the literature, security inks made from photoluminescent materials are the most widely investigated. The security inks suffer from the drawbacks such as lack of universality on various substrates. In this work, we provide a promising new complement to the security ink-based protection techniques. We report a kind of portable photoluminescent chalk made from lanthanide-doped ultralong hydroxyapatite nanowires, which can directly write the covert information at anytime and anyplace on arbitrary surfaces and substrates including paper sheets, metals, fabrics, plastics, woods, walls, foams, leaves, and even human body. The as-written original information cannot be recognized under the ambient light, but it is readable upon exposure to the ultraviolet light. In this work, the dual and triple encryption strategies are also provided for the high-level information protection. It is expected that the versatile, convenient, and user-friendly strategy demonstrated herein is promising for the on-site secret information protection.

ce:section.ce:para: In many spy movies, it is a common scene that the secret agent hides the secret information by a specific encryption technique, which can securely communicate with people on one’s own side and avoid the leakage of the secret information to opponents [1]. Beyond those fictitious movies, the confidential information protection is of great significance for secure communication in the current information era. The strategy for protecting the confidential information involves three components: materials, encryption and decryption techniques. As for materials used to store the secret graphical or textual information, it is desirable that materials are low-cost, and can be fabricated on a large scale [2], and they have superior physical and chemical stability to prevent non-artificial destruction such as photobleaching of organic dyes [3]. As for encryption techniques, it is necessary that the hidden information is difficult to be recognized under normal conditions [4]. In addition, the decryption techniques should be convenient and rapid for the reading of the original information with portable and inexpensive equipment [5].

In the past decades, efforts have been devoted to developing advanced technologies to enhance the capabilities of these three components, such as holograms [6], molecular coding [7], stimuli-responsive color-changing systems [8], and security inks [9,10]. However, these approaches have drawbacks which limit their practical applications. For example, holograms involve the complex photochemical process, high-cost chemicals and instruments [11]. Although molecular coding can provide multiple levels of information protection, the encrypted information can only be recognized by the highly skilled analytical expertise using the specialized equipments [1,7], and the direct and straightforward readout of encrypted information is difficult.

Recently, inspired by Nature, stimuli-responsive color-changing systems have attracted much attention owing to their advantages such as durability, absence of pigment photochemical degradation, and special encryption and decryption approaches [8]. The stimuli, such as light [12,13], vapor [14,15], solvent [2], mechanical force [16], electrical field [17,18], and temperature [19], are needed to trigger the change of structure and thus the color. These stimuli can be accurately adjusted to run the color-changing systems in the laboratory. However, the harsh outside environment conditions may not allow them to function well as in the laboratory conditions.

Among various strategies used for the secret information protection in the previous reports, security inks made from photoluminescent (PL) materials are the most widely studied, including those that show down- or up-conversion photoluminescence and phosphorescence [20–27]. The attractive advantages of the security inks include that: (1) PL materials can be synthesized in environmentally friendly and facile ways, making low-cost and large-scale production possible [28,29]; (2) security inks are compatible with printing techniques such as screen and inkjet printing [30–35], the words and patterns can be printed with high resolution; and (3) the hidden information printed with security inks can be decrypted using portable devices such as mobile phone or ultraviolet (UV) light [20,36,37]. However, there are two problems associated with security inks. First, the optimization for ink formulations is necessary for high-quality printing [5,10,32], and some factors should take into account such as the concentration, viscosity, dispersibility and stability of PL materials in the suitable ink medium [33,35,38]. Second, the printing techniques are not universally applicable on various surfaces and substrates, and the printing devices such as the inkjet printer are not convenient to carry for the outside usage [4,35,39].

Herein, we report a new kind of PL chalk made from lanthanide (Ln)-doped ultralong hydroxyapatite (HAP) nanowires as a promising new complement to security inks (Scheme 1 a). The as-prepared PL chalk is lightweight and miniature. By doping with different lanthanide ions, the fluorescence color of the PL chalk can be adjusted. The users can directly write covert information on arbitrary surfaces and substrates using the PL chalk, including paper sheets, metals, fabrics, plastics, woods, walls, foams, leaves, and even human body (Scheme 1b). The as-written secret information can be rapidly read with the help of a hand-held UV light. In addition, dual and triple encryption strategies are also demonstrated for high-level information protection (Scheme 1c).

CaCl2 and NaH2PO4·2H2O were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Sodium oleate, TbCl3·6H2O, and EuCl3·6H2O were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation. All chemicals were used as received without further purification. Deionized water was used in all experiments.

Ultralong HAP nanowires were synthesized based on the calcium oleate precursor solvothermal method developed by this research group [40]. Briefly, CaCl2 (2.220 g) aqueous solution (250 mL) and NaH2PO4·2H2O (2.808 g) aqueous solution (250 mL) were separately added in sodium oleate (24.355 g) aqueous solution (250 mL). The resulting mixture was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave (1 L), sealed, and subjected to the thermal treatment at 200 °C for 36 h. Ultralong HAP nanowires were washed with ethanol and deionized water several times, and then dried by freeze-drying for further use.

The synthesis procedure for lanthanide (Ln)-doped HAP nanowires was similar to that for undoped HAP nanowires. The initial concentrations of NaH2PO4·2H2O and sodium oleate were unchanged. 2.109 g CaCl2 and 0.366 g EuCl3·6H2O were used for the synthesis of HAP:Eu3+ nanowires, and 2.109 g CaCl2 and 0.373 g TbCl3·6H2O were used for the synthesis of HAP:Tb3+ nanowires. The Ln/(Ca + Ln) molar ratio was 5%.

A small volume of absolute ethanol was added into a powder (300 mg) of HAP:Eu3+ or HAP:Tb3+ nanowires, and then the mixture was subjected to grinding. The obtained wet powder was loaded into a cylindrical steel mold and pressed into a preforming chalk. The shape and size of the as-prepared chalk depended on the steel mold. After drying at 60 °C, the preforming chalk was thermally treated from room temperature to 1000 °C with a heating rate of 5 °C min−1 and maintained at that temperature for 2 h.

The samples were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Magellan 400, FEI, USA) equipped with an X-MAXN energy dispersive X-ray detector (Oxford Instrument), transmission electron microscopy (TEM, H-800, Hitachi, Japan), X-ray diffraction (XRD, D/max 2550 V, Rigaku, Japan) with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.54178 Å), Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (FTIR-7600, Lambda Scientific, Australia), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250Xi, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) with Al Kα radiation. The thermogravimetric curves were recorded on a thermal analyzer (STA 409PC, Netzsch, Germany) with a heating rate of 10 °C min−1 in flowing air. Photoluminescence spectra were obtained on a fluorescence spectrometer (F-4600, Hitachi, Japan). The fluorescence lifetime spectroscopy and quantum yield were measured on a photoluminescence spectrometer with an integrating sphere (FLS-1000, Edinburgh Instruments, UK). Before the determination of quantum yield, the total photons were obtained by exposing the blank container to the excitation source. Then, the emission photons and unabsorbed photons were obtained by exposing the sample to the excitation source. The quantum yield was determined based on the ratio of emission and absorbed photons.

Chalk is a common writing tool with a long history and widely used in school education owing to easy writing by hand and low-cost production, which plays an important role in the education of knowledge. In this work, taking advantage of photoluminescent lanthanide-doped HAP nanowires, we demonstrate a new kind of PL chalk that can emit brilliant red or green fluorescence under the UV light (Scheme 1). The chalk is prepared by pressure molding of the Ln-doped HAP nanowires, followed by thermal treatment (Fig. S1 in the Supporting Information).

The as-prepared Ln-doped HAP nanowires are an ideal building material for the construction of the PL chalk due to the following reasons: (1) various PL materials, including organic dyes [41], quantum dots [42–44], metal-organic frameworks [38,45], and Ln-based materials [46–48], have been developed, among which Ln-based materials have superior advantages over other PL materials, such as pure fluorescence color, sharp emission, high stability, and high quantum yield; (2) the high stability of the as-prepared PL chalk is necessary for practical applications, and HAP materials are considered as excellent candidates for constructing lightweight and high-strength architectures [49,50]; (3) HAP is regarded as a good matrix for lanthanide element doping owing to its ion exchange capability [51–53]; (4) the synthesis process of HAP nanowires is simple and can be scaled up for large-scale production [54–56].

Ultralong HAP nanowires with lengths ranging from tens of micrometers up to >100 µm were synthesized by the calcium oleate precursor solvothermal method [40], as shown in Fig. 1 a. In this process, sodium oleate acts as a structure-directing agent to guide the growth of HAP along the c axis. Calcium oleate formed by the chemical reaction between sodium oleate and calcium salt plays roles of calcium source and precursor. The oleate groups adsorb on the surface of the as-prepared HAP nanowires. The as-synthesized HAP nanowires prefer to assemble into bundles along the longitudinal direction. The HAP nanowires and bundles interweave with each other to form a porous network structure (Fig. 1a). Taking advantage of ion exchange capability, HAP nanowires are used as a host material for lanthanide element doping to obtain photoluminescent lanthanide-doped HAP nanowires. Owing to the similar ionic radii of calcium and lanthanide ions, the calcium ions of HAP nanowires can be partially substituted by lanthanide ions (Eu3+ and Tb3+ ions are used in this work, denoted as HAP:Eu3+ and HAP:Tb3+ nanowires, respectively) [51]. Doping lanthanide ions with a Ln/(Ca + Ln) molar ratio of 5% has no obvious influence on the morphology of the as-prepared nanowires, as shown in Fig. 1b and c. However, the excess amount of doped lanthanide ions would cause a decrease in the aspect ratio of HAP nanowires [57].

The preforming PL chalk was fabricated by pressure molding of HAP:Eu3+ or HAP:Tb3+ nanowires in the steel mold (Fig. S1 in the Supporting Information). It is worth noting that the shape and size of the as-prepared PL chalk depend on the shape and size of the steel mold. In the preforming PL chalk, ultralong HAP:Eu3+ or HAP:Tb3+ nanowires with high aspect ratios interweave with each other to form a porous network architecture (Fig. 1d–f). This feature of HAP:Eu3+ or HAP:Tb3+ nanowires is distinctive compared with low-aspect-ratio nanocrystals, and can enhance the mechanical properties and prevent the collapse of the PL chalk [58,59]. In this regard, the amount of doped lanthanide ions should be relatively low in order to maintain the high aspect ratios of the as-prepared nanowires. As shown in Fig. 1e and f, the microstructure of the preforming Ln-doped PL chalk is similar to that of the undoped one. To further improve the mechanical properties, the preformed PL chalk was subjected to sintering at 1000 °C. The as-prepared PL chalk exhibits a similar white color and shape to the commercial chalk (Fig. S1 in the Supporting Information). However, the microstructure of the PL chalk after sintering at 1000 °C has an obvious change compared with that of the sample without sintering (Fig. 1g–i). It seems that ultralong HAP:Eu3+ or HAP:Tb3+ nanowires break to form worm-like particles (Fig. S2 in the Supporting Information).

The as-prepared ultralong nanowires of HAP, HAP:Eu3+ and HAP:Tb3+, and the corresponding chalks after sintering were further characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS). As shown in Fig. 2 a, the diffraction peaks of ultralong HAP nanowires can be indexed to the single crystal phase of hydroxyapatite (JCPDF 74-0566). The broadening of the diffraction peaks in the XRD pattern can be explained by the nanometer-sized diameters of the as-prepared ultralong HAP nanowires. After doping Eu3+ and Tb3+ ions, the crystal structure of the host HAP nanowires has no obvious change (Fig. 2a). However, the small shifting of diffraction peaks to higher 2θ angle is observed (Fig. S3 in the Supporting Information), indicating the shrinkage of the crystal lattice to some extent. Compared with Ca2+ ions, smaller ionic radii and higher oxidation state of both Eu3+ and Tb3+ ions may be responsible for such peak shifting [51]. The shifting of diffraction peaks also suggests that lanthanide ions are successfully incorporated into the crystal lattice of HAP nanowires by partially replacing Ca2+ ions.

Fig. 2b shows the FTIR spectra of the as-prepared ultralong nanowires of HAP, HAP:Eu3+, and HAP:Tb3+. The absorption peaks at 1099, 1028, 962, 604, 561 cm−1 are assigned to the PO4 3− group. A broad absorption peak at about 3429 cm−1 together with a peak at 1637 cm−1 correspond to the adsorbed water molecules. During the synthetic process, the oleate groups adsorb on the surface of ultralong HAP nanowires, and the characteristic peaks of the oleate group are located at 2925 and 2854 cm−1 for the asymmetric and symmetric CH2 stretching vibration, and at 1560 and 1458 cm−1 for the asymmetric and symmetric COO− stretching vibration [56].

In the XPS spectra (Fig. 2c), the P 2p, Ca 2p, and O 1s peaks of the as-prepared ultralong HAP nanowires shift to higher binding energy after doping lanthanide ions, suggesting that the coordination chemistry of P, Ca, and O atoms is changed. Again, the XPS analysis results confirm the successful incorporation of Eu3+ and Tb3+ ions into the HAP crystal lattice.

After the preformed HAP, HAP:Eu3+, and HAP:Tb3+ chalks undergo the high-temperature sintering, the chalks can maintain the integrity and shape due to the high thermal stability of HAP, HAP:Eu3+, and HAP:Tb3+ nanowires (Fig. S4 in the Supporting Information). The crystal phases of the as-prepared HAP, HAP:Eu3+, and HAP:Tb3+ chalks after sintering were investigated. Fig. 3 a shows the XRD patterns of the as-prepared HAP, HAP:Eu3+, and HAP:Tb3+ chalks after sintering at 1000 °C, and two major changes are observed. First, in addition to the characteristic diffraction peaks of the HAP crystal phase, the additional peaks that can be indexed to the Ca3(PO4)2 crystal phase appear (Fig. S5 in the Supporting Information), indicating that the partial phase transformation from hydroxyapatite to Ca3(PO4)2 occurs during the sintering process [49]. Second, the widths of diffraction peaks of the HAP, HAP:Eu3+, and HAP:Tb3+ chalks after sintering in the XRD patterns become narrower than those of the samples before sintering (Figs. 2a and 3a), indicating the increased diameters and crystallinity of the building blocks after sintering. The previous work found a strong correlation between fluorescence properties and crystallinity of materials [60], which showed that the crystallization could enhance the fluorescence intensity. The similar experimental results are also found in this work. As a result, sintering plays two roles in the formation of the PL chalk, that is, enhance both the mechanical and fluorescence properties.

In addition, sintering results in the decomposition of adsorbed oleate groups on the surface of ulltralong nanowires, thus, the absence of characteristic absorption peaks of oleate groups in the FTIR spectra, as shown in Fig. 3b. Fig. 3c–e shows the EDS spectra of the as-prepared HAP, HAP:Eu3+, and HAP:Tb3+ chalks after sintering at 1000 °C. P, Ca, and O elements are detected in the EDS spectrum of the HAP chalk, but no lanthanide element is found. In comparison, Eu and Tb elements are found in the HAP:Eu3+ and HAP:Tb3+ chalks, respectively, which confirms the successful doping of the lanthanide ions in the HAP crystal lattice.

The photoluminescent properties of the as-prepared PL chalks after sintering at 1000 °C were further studied. As shown in Fig. 4 a and b, the most intense excitation wavelength is determined to be 393 nm and 377 nm for the HAP:Eu3+ chalk and HAP:Tb3+ chalk, respectively. The photoluminescence emission spectra of the HAP:Eu3+ chalk and HAP:Tb3+ chalk were recorded at the excitation wavelength of 393 nm and 377 nm, respectively. The emission spectrum of the HAP:Eu3+ chalk is shown in Fig. 4c. Four emission peaks at 576, 593, 616, and 655 nm are observed, which are attributed to the typical 5D0 → 7F J (J = 0, 1, 2, 3) transitions of Eu3+ ions [51,60]. Among these photoluminescence emission peaks, the strongest one is located at 616 nm, indicating that the HAP:Eu3+ chalk would emit red fluorescence upon exposure to the UV light. The similar photoluminescence behavior is also observed for the HAP:Tb3+ chalk with the emission peaks in the range from 475 to 650 nm (Fig. 4d). There are four characteristic emission peaks at 489, 545, 590, and 623 nm corresponding to the 5D4 → 7F J (J = 6, 5, 4, 3) transitions of Tb3+ ion [53]. The maximum photoluminescence intensity is observed at 545 nm, indicating the emission of green fluorescence of the HAP:Eu3+ chalk under the UV light.

The effect of sintering on the photoluminescence properties was investigated. The photoluminescence emission spectra of the HAP:Eu3+ and HAP:Tb3+ PL chalks before sintering were recorded (Fig. S6 in the Supporting Information). It has been found that the strongest emission peak at 616 nm of the HAP:Eu3+ chalk after sintering is much higher than that of the HAP:Eu3+ chalk before sintering (Fig. S6a in the Supporting Information). The similar experimental result is also observed for the HAP:Tb3+ chalk for the strongest emission peak at 545 nm (Fig. S6b in the Supporting Information). The fluorescence decay of the HAP:Eu3+ and HAP:Tb3+ chalks before and after sintering was further investigated (Fig. S7 in the Supporting Information). The fluorescence lifetime of the HAP:Eu3+ chalk and HAP:Tb3+ chalk before sintering is 0.53 and 0.94 ms, respectively, which significantly increases to 2.20 and 1.93 ms after sintering (Table S1 in the Supporting Information). In addition, the quantum yield is determined to be 24.97% and 18.90% for the HAP:Eu3+ chalk and HAP:Tb3+ chalk after sintering, respectively, whereas the value is only 2.07% and 3.03% for the HAP:Eu3+ chalk and HAP:Tb3+ chalk before sintering, respectively (Table S1 in the Supporting Information). The experimental results indicate that the sintering treatment is an effective strategy to significantly enhance the fluorescence properties of the HAP:Eu3+ and HAP:Tb3+ PL chalks.

The as-prepared the HAP:Eu3+ and HAP:Tb3+ PL chalks have a similar white color and shape to the commercial chalk, as shown in Fig. 5 a. Under irradiation with a hand-held UV light with a wavelength of 254 nm, the HAP:Eu3+ and HAP:Tb3+ PL chalks exhibit brilliant red and green fluorescence, respectively (Fig. 5b). For the purpose of the convenient usage and multifunction, a new kind of double PL chalk by using HAP:Eu3+ nanowires and then HAP:Tb3+ nanowires, followed by pressure molding and sintering. As shown in Fig. 5c and d, the upper part of the double PL chalk is made from HAP:Tb3+ nanowires, and emits green fluorescence, whereas the lower part of the double PL chalk consists of HAP:Eu3+ nanowires, and emits red color. The most desirable features of the PL chalk are portable and writable. As shown in Fig. 5e, the PL chalk can be packed into the sleeve, which is convenient for users to carry for outside usage. Furthermore, owing to easy writing of the PL chalk, the users can directly write the information on any substrate or surface (Fig. 5f). The portable and writable traits of the PL chalk make the information protection possible at anytime and anywhere.

The commercial chalk has been a popular handwriting tool for a long history. Its working mechanism is that when the users write the information on the substrate with the chalk, the handwriting can be well reserved for reading owing to the strong physical attachment of the powder from the chalk to the substrate. Similar to the commercial chalk, the as-prepared PL chalk reported in this work exhibits high compatibility with various substrates. The PL chalk is writable on arbitrary substrates and surfaces. As shown in Fig. 6 , English letters can be written using the PL chalk on arbitrary substrates, including paper, metal, fabric, plastic, wood, wall, foam, leaf, and even human hand. These substrates include artificial (metal and plastic), natural (leaf), flat (paper), curved (human hand), soft (paper, fabric), and rigid (metal, wood, wall) ones. Upon exposure to the UV light, the information written on the substrates by the PL chalk exhibits intense fluorescence that is visible by naked eye. It should be mentioned that the PL chalk with a white color is much more suitable for writing on the substrates with a white color such as paper, metal, fabric, plastic, wall, and foam. In these cases, the written information is almost invisible, which can protect the secret information. For the substrates with dark colors like wood and leaf, the handwriting is visible. In order to improve the security of information on such substrates, we also provide the rational design strategy, as shown in Fig. 7 .

By using the as-prepared HAP:Eu3+ and HAP:Tb3+ chalks, the secret information with multicolor fluorescence under the UV light, which is designable, can be written on the substrates with a dark background (Fig. 7). Taking a photoluminescent flower as a typical example (Fig. 7a), in the encryption process, the leaves of the flower are written with the HAP:Tb3+ chalk, whereas the HAP:Eu3+ chalk is adopted to write the petals of the flower. The as-written flower displays a white color under the ambient light, and the hidden information cannot be read directly. However, exposure of the as-written flower to the UV light results in the clear display of the red petals and green leaves with a sharp color contrast (Fig. 7a). As a second example, a route is hidden in a complex map drawn by the as-prepared PL chalks (Fig. 7b), and it is impossible to recognize the right route under the ambient light because they are all in a white color. However, the hidden route written with the HAP:Eu3+ chalk emits red fluorescence under the UV light, which can be clearly distinguished from other green lines (Fig. 7b). As a third example, the numbers from “0” to “9” are written with the HAP:Eu3+ chalk, after which the lines are written with the HAP:Tb3+ chalk to generate ten number “8”, and these numbers exhibit a white color to show ten “8” under the ambient light, and the hidden information cannot be seen by the naked eye. Under the UV light, however, the numbers from “0” to “9” written with the HAP:Eu3+ chalk exhibit a red color and can be see clearly (Fig. 7c). These fake number “8” is able to mislead people and hide the secret information under the ambient light.

We also propose dual-encrypted and triple-encrypted strategies for high-level confidential information protection. As shown in Fig. 7d, the words “HELLO THERE” are dual-encrypted by scrambled letters, and are written as “HTEHLELROE”, and the “HELLO” part is written with the HAP:Eu3+ chalk, whereas the “THERE” part is written with the HAP:Tb3+ chalk. The encrypted information exhibits a white color and is totally meaningless under the ambient light. The original information only can be read by irradiation with a UV light and then adjust the sequence of English letters in a correct way. The triple encryption of the confidential information can be achieved with the aid of the ASCII binary codes [61]. As shown in Fig. 7e, a two-color microarray is generated using the double PL chalk, which exhibits a white color under the ambient light. However, the green and red fluorescent dots can be clearly seen under the UV light, which represents “1” and “0”, respectively. The decryption process is divided into three steps. First, the white dots under the ambient light exhibit their own fluorescence upon exposure to the UV light. Second, the dots with different colors are transformed into number “1” or “0”. Third, these binary codes can be translated into the corresponding letters according to the ASCII codes. In this work, using the triple-encrypted strategy, the encrypted information “SICCAS”, which is an acronym of “Shanghai Institute of Ceramics, Chinese Academy of Sciences”, can be decrypted. The multiple encryption can improve the security level for the confidential information protection.

In this work, we have demonstrated a new kind of the PL chalk made from lanthanide-doped hydroxyapatite ultralong nanowires. The preparation process of the PL chalk, including the synthesis of lanthanide-doped hydroxyapatite ultralong nanowires, pressure molding and sintering, is facile and efficient, and may be scaled up for a large-scale production. The fluorescence color of the PL chalk can be well controlled by adjusting different doped lanthanide ions. The lightweight and miniature PL chalk is portable and writable, and the users can directly write the covert information on arbitrary substrates and surfaces with the PL chalk at anytime and anywhere. The as-written confidential information exhibits a white color, and cannot be recognized under the ambient light, while it is readable immediately upon exposure to the UV light. We have also proposed the strategy for data encryption using the multicolor PL chalk. High-security secret information protection has been designed based on the proposed dual encryption and triple encryption strategies.

In spite of advantages of the as-prepared PL chalk, it should be mentioned that there are some barriers for the PL chalk to be overcome. For example, (1) it is difficult to write small-sized words with high resolution using the PL chalk; (2) the as-written information is visible on the substrates with a dark background; and (3) how to write the confidential information on a large number of substrates using the PL chalk is another problem. Nevertheless, the portable and writable PL chalk demonstrated herein is a promising complement to the widely-used security ink encryption technique. It is expected that the new kind of PL chalk reported in this work will find applications in various fields such as on-site secret information protection, anti-counterfeiting, item checking and inspection.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21601199, 21875277), the Science and Technology Commission of Shanghai Municipality (18ZR1445200), Shanghai Sailing Program (16YF1413000) is gratefully acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2019.03.153.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
